# Deep dive on management and the setup — Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena (BMPS.MI)

*Two consecutive earnings calls (Q3/9M 2025 on 2025-11-07; Q4/FY2025 preliminary on 2026-02-10), cross-referenced against the screener's multi-year financial trajectory. The defining event spanning both calls is the transformational acquisition of Mediobanca — MPS, the 553-year-old serially-bailed-out Sienese bank, has turned predator and swallowed Italy's premier investment bank and wealth manager.*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The arc across these two calls is a single, continuous victory lap with the volume rising. In **Q3 2025 (Nov-07)**, the story is *"we did the impossible — the Mediobanca tender closed at 86.3% acceptance"* — Lovaglio frames it as *"a landmark moment… we are opening a new chapter in 553 years history of Monte Paschi."* The emphasis is on **execution proof** (the deal cleared, governance secured *"from day 1"*) and on the **standalone engine still humming** despite the distraction (*"our people were not distracted… they continue to serve clients, deliver strong commercial momentum"*).

By **Q4/FY 2025 (Feb-10)**, the narrative expands from "we did the deal" to *"here is the combined entity, and it is bigger than we promised."* Lovaglio elevates the language to *"a decisive turning point that sets the direction of the group for the years to come… not forecasts, not expectation, but tangible evidence."* The combined-group FY2025 net profit of **EUR 3.0bn before purchase-price-allocation accounting** (EUR 2.716bn reported) is the new headline, and management pre-announces a **EUR 700m savings** target *"with a potential upside"* and a **10% dividend yield** (EUR 0.86 per share, more than EUR 2.6bn total). The growth drivers introduced and amplified: (1) **wealth management** (gross inflows +17-18% year over year, now about one-third of revenues — the wedge for the re-rating), (2) **Compass / consumer finance international expansion**, and (3) the **deferred-tax-asset write-up** monetizing the bank's tax-loss legacy (about EUR 500m per year for six years of core-capital support).

What is quietly *introduced but deferred*: every hard number on the phasing of savings, the integration-charge split, and the monetization of the Generali stake is pushed to the **February 27 Capital Markets Day**. Both calls are, in effect, a runway to that single forcing event — which has since fired (see section 5). Nothing material is *dropped*; rather, the standalone net-interest-income story (declining after the rate peak) is de-emphasized in favor of the fee and wealth-management story that carries the re-rating case.

## 2. CLAIMS VERSUS FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Where it appears | The hard evidence | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| *"Strong stand-alone results… net profit up 17.5-17.7% YoY"* | Both calls | FY2025 net income **EUR 3.04bn** (screener) versus EUR 1.95bn FY2024 — but the screener also shows **earnings per share of EUR 1.54, flat** year over year (-0.6%), and the year-over-year profit jump is *heavily tax-driven* (EUR 961m deferred-tax write-up). | **Half-true.** Operating progress is real (net operating profit +6.4%, cost-to-income ratio stable at 46%), but the headline net-income growth is **inflated by a one-off deferred-tax write-up**, not recurring earnings. Earnings per share are flat. |
| *"Resilient revenues sustained by fees"* offsetting net-interest-income decline | Both calls | Screener: **revenue -27.8% year over year** (FY2025 EUR 4.74bn versus EUR 6.56bn FY2024); fee income +8.2% for the full year. | **Mixed/concerning.** Fees genuinely grew, but the top line *fell 28%* as net interest income normalized off the rate peak. The "resilience" claim papers over a steep revenue contraction. The 3-year revenue growth rate is only +6.6%. |
| *"Best-in-class capital… core capital ratio (CET1) 16.2-16.9% including Mediobanca"* | Both calls | The screener's net-debt fields are unreliable for a bank; **live correction: net debt EUR 12.89bn** (total debt EUR 45.29bn minus cash EUR 32.40bn as of 2026-03-31). | **Credible on regulatory capital** (the core capital ratio is the right bank metric, and above 16% after the deal is genuinely strong). The screener's "-3.83B net cash" is a non-bank artifact and must be ignored — this is a leveraged balance sheet by construction. |
| *"One of the highest dividend yields in European banking — 10%"* | Q4 call | EUR 0.86 per share, more than EUR 2.6bn total, on a roughly EUR 10-11 share. | **True and now spent** — the dividend was **paid 2026-05-20** (ex-date 05-18). The yield headline that anchored the bull case is in the rear-view mirror. |
| *"EUR 700m savings… with potential upside"* | Q4 call | No segment-level proof yet; *"bottom-up analysis confirmed the outside-in estimate."* | **Unproven assertion** at call date — deferred to the February 27 Capital Markets Day. (That day has since confirmed EUR 700m plus a EUR 3.7bn 2030 profit target; see section 5.) |
| Return on equity 12.6% / return on invested capital 2.4% | Screener | Return on invested capital of **2.4%** is far below Intesa Sanpaolo (about 9%) and UniCredit (about 12%); the 64% net margin is a provisioning-reversal plus deferred-tax artifact. | **The quality gap is real.** Returns are *below* peer banks despite the optical margin. Piotroski score 4/9, Altman-Z -0.4 (the latter is a non-meaningful metric for a bank). |

**Net:** the story is *richer than the recurring numbers justify.* Operating momentum (fees, wealth-management inflows, cost discipline, falling cost of risk to 40 basis points) is genuine, but the marquee "profit up about 18%" and "EUR 3bn group profit" headlines lean heavily on a **non-recurring EUR 961m deferred-tax write-up** and obscure a **-28% revenue contraction**. Earnings per share are dead flat (EUR 1.54), and the 5-year earnings-per-share growth rate is **-30.3%**, book value per share **-51.1%** — the legacy of dilutive recapitalizations.

## 3. TONE AND CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: rising → already near-euphoric.** This is an offensive, expansion-mode management, not a defensive one.

- **Q3 2025:** *"This is a landmark moment… a clear endorsement… accretive financially, strategically and commercially."* Confident, but still framed around *execution* (the deal closing).
- **Q4 2025:** Tone escalates to triumphalism: *"a decisive turning point… tangible evidence, not forecasts… one of the most attractive projects for the banking sector."* Lovaglio openly spars with analysts on valuation — when BofA's Antonio Reale asks *"how can you convince the market that it's wrong?"*, Lovaglio answers *"our evaluation… is absolutely underestimated today."*

The shift is decisively toward **offensive, growth language** (expand Compass abroad, exploit savings, *"further reward our shareholders"*) and *away* from the defensive, macro framing typical of a turnaround bank. The only conservative note is deliberate sandbagging on the Generali stake and the Danish-Compromise benefit (*"we like to be very conservative… all the figures are without this benefit"*) — confidence dressed as prudence.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: high** on near-term delivery, **unproven** on the multi-year savings plan.

- In Q3, management set FY2025 pretax guidance to *"well exceed EUR 1.6bn"*; in Q4 the standalone pretax printed **EUR 1.7bn, "well above guidance."** That is a clean **over-deliver** inside one quarter.
- The screener corroborates an **80% earnings-beat rate (4 of 5 quarters)** and *"beat 4/5 qtrs."*
- Standalone net operating profit +6.4%, cost-to-income ratio held at 46%, cost of risk *improved* from 53 to 40 basis points — all delivered as flagged.
- The EUR 700m savings / February 27 plan was a *promise* at call date; it has since been **confirmed and exceeded in ambition** at the Capital Markets Day (EUR 3.7bn 2030 profit, 100% capital return through 2030). Management's pattern is to under-promise on dated near-term numbers and over-deliver — a **sandbagging-positive** signal.

**Score: medium-high.** Near-term guidance is reliably beaten; the long-range 2030 plan is credible-but-unproven and depends on integration execution and Generali optionality not yet in hand.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

Analysts circled three recurring sore spots, all of which management **deferred to February 27**:

1. **Integration-charge magnitude and phasing** (Equita's Lisi, KBW's Cruz): *"how many years… what split between Mediobanca and Monte Paschi standalone?"* — Maffezzoni: *"this is a second-level details question… let us answer on the 27th of February."* The roughly EUR 500-600m gross integration cost was acknowledged but its timing left vague.
2. **Excess-capital deployment** (Intermonte's Giacometti): dividend versus buyback versus M&A — Lovaglio kept it optional (*"if it's through buyback or extraordinary dividend… something we evaluate time to time"*). The valuation-re-rating case is management's own pet topic — Lovaglio volunteers it unprompted ("this part of business deserve a significant rerating").
3. **The Generali stake** (Equita, Autonomous): how and when to monetize the inherited roughly 13% Generali holding and the more-than-50-basis-point Danish-Compromise capital benefit — repeatedly punted (*"honestly, it's difficult to predict when this can be completed… it depends not exclusively on us"*).

The most telling exchange: BNP's Ulargui asked whether MPS would do a **BPER-style total-return swap on its own shares** (a tell that a *third party could still bid for MPS, or the float is in play*) — Lovaglio declined flatly (*"we are focused on what we know better, that is doing banking"*). Analysts are not worried about a *bad* outcome; they are pushing for the *quantification* of an outcome management has already declared good. That is a high-quality problem to have, but it also means **the upside is now a "show me the savings" execution story, not a discovery story.**

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

Green flags:
- **Mediobanca tender closed at 86.3%** (Q3) — clean governance from day one, the central execution risk retired.
- **Standalone over-delivery** — pretax EUR 1.7bn versus a "more than EUR 1.6bn" guide; cost-to-income ratio 46%, cost of risk 40 basis points, non-performing-exposure ratio 3.5% gross / 1.8% net with 49.3% coverage (Q4). Asset quality genuinely improved.
- **Fee and wealth-management engine** — wealth-management gross inflows +17% to EUR 17bn for the year; about one-third of revenue now fee-based — the structural wedge for the re-rating and the most durable part of the story.
- **Deferred-tax monetization** — EUR 961m full-year write-up plus about EUR 500m per year of core-capital support for six years; a real, if non-recurring, capital lever.
- **10% dividend paid** (now spent — see red flag).

Red flags:
- **Revenue -27.8% year over year** — the top line contracted sharply as net interest income normalized; "resilient revenue" is doing heavy lifting.
- **Headline net income is tax-engineered** — the EUR 3bn group / +18% standalone profit leans on the EUR 961m deferred-tax write-up; **earnings per share are flat at EUR 1.54**, the 5-year earnings-per-share growth rate is -30.3%, and book value per share -51.1%.
- **Return on invested capital 2.4%** — well below Intesa Sanpaolo and UniCredit; the bank is *cheaper for a reason* on returns even after the re-rating.
- **The two load-bearing catalysts have fired** — the February 27 Capital Markets Day happened and the EUR 0.86 dividend was paid 2026-05-20. The events that drove the +31%-versus-200-day, about-98%-of-52-week-range run are **in the rear-view mirror.**
- **Trailing-twelve-month free cash flow -EUR 2.68bn / latest-quarter earnings per share -48% year over year** — free cash flow is deeply negative on a trailing basis (balance-sheet expansion from consolidating Mediobanca distorts this, but it is a flag) and the most recent quarter's earnings per share fell 48% year over year off the deferred-tax-boosted comparison.
- **Integration risk now begins** — EUR 600m of charges land mostly in 2026; private-banker retention at Mediobanca is the classic risk of value leaking away in a contested takeover of a premier wealth-management franchise.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **From "turnaround bank" to "wealth-manager re-rating story."** The single most important terminology shift across the calls is the relentless repetition that *"almost 1/3 of revenues coming from asset gathering and Wealth Management"* — Lovaglio is explicitly coaching the market to apply a **wealth-management multiple** (high-teens price-to-earnings) to a third of the business rather than a distressed-bank multiple to all of it. That is the entire bull case in one sentence.
- **"Conservative" as a confidence flex.** Management twice excludes the Generali / Danish-Compromise benefit (more than 50 basis points) from all figures, then asserts *"we deserve it… it should come to us."* The sandbagging is real but it also signals there is *more* capital optionality than the headline core capital ratio of 16.2% suggests.
- **The Compass-abroad ambition** is new and underappreciated — Lovaglio is most animated (*"what is for me even more exciting"*) about exporting Mediobanca's Compass consumer-finance scoring engine outside Italy. This is the one *organic growth* leg that is not yet in any number.
- **Lovaglio personally owns the valuation argument** — he raises the "we're undervalued" point unprompted in both calls and in the analyst Q&A, an unusual degree of CEO promotion that suggests management views *closing the discount* as a primary objective.
- **The rejected total-return swap / no-buyback-derivative answer** quietly closes the door on MPS itself being a target — it is now firmly the consolidator, not the consolidated.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: strengthening — but the easy money has been made.** This is a genuine balance-sheet-and-franchise improvement story: a serially-bailed-out bank that retired its execution risk (86.3% tender), monetized its deferred-tax legacy, paid a 10% yield, holds a core capital ratio above 16%, and acquired a premier wealth-management and corporate-and-investment-banking franchise that justifies a structurally higher multiple. The standalone engine over-delivered. The trajectory is up.

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** *the catalysts that drove the re-rating are spent.* The stock is at about 98% of its 52-week range, up 31% versus its 200-day average, the February 27 Capital Markets Day has fired (and confirmed the bull plan), and the headline dividend was paid 2026-05-20. The screener's own margin of safety is **-10%** (price 10.96 versus fair value 9.13), discounted cash flow margin of safety -12%, earnings power value +1%, intrinsic-value-15 method -100% — only owner-earnings (+52%, distorted by the deferred-tax-inflated earnings base) and Graham (+22%) flatter it. **You are no longer buying a discovery; you are buying execution of a fully-disclosed 2026-2030 integration plan, at the high, with return on invested capital (2.4%) still below peers and the next hard catalyst (merger completion / proof of savings) not due until the second half of 2026 through 2028.** Size it as a *fairly-valued, post-catalyst hold*, not as an asymmetric special situation.

**Economic moat.** Moat type: **regulatory license plus intangible brand and distribution** (a domestic Italian banking license with a 553-year deposit franchise and, now, Mediobanca's premier wealth-management and corporate-and-investment-banking brand). Width, judged on the *returns* evidence: **return on invested capital 2.4%** is below the cost of capital and far below Intesa Sanpaolo and UniCredit — this is a **narrow** moat at best. The *trend* is genuinely **widening** (improving cost of risk, fee-mix shift to wealth management, scale from the merger, cost-to-income ratio held at 46% — the franchise is getting better, not worse). A bank's moat is real but commodity-ish; the Mediobanca wealth-management and corporate-and-investment-banking addition is the most defensible piece.

**Secular threat.** The structural force is **payments and wealth fintech disintermediation** of legacy banking — but for a deposit-funded universal bank pivoting *into* advised wealth management (the stickiest, least-disintermediated banking product), the threat is **manageable**, not terminal. Italian banking is consolidating *toward* scale, not being structurally hollowed out, and wealth-management advice is on the right side of the fintech trend. There is no melting terminal base here.

**Mispricing versus value trap:** this is **neither a fear-print nor a value trap** — it is a *substantially-completed re-rating.* The cheapness that existed 18 months ago has been arbitraged away by the market correctly recognizing the franchise improvement and the Mediobanca prize. At about 98% of the 52-week range with a -10% screener margin of safety, the asymmetry is gone; what remains is fair value plus modest savings upside against integration-execution and Italian-macro / BTP-spread sensitivity. A quality improvement that the price already reflects.

Management credibility 4 (out of 5). Trajectory: strengthening. Moat: narrow. Moat trend: widening. Secular threat: manageable. of 5.
